Here are pytest-style unit tests (no special formatting):

from truncate_module import truncate
import pytest

def test_truncate_happy_path():
    s = "Hello World"
    result = truncate(s, 5)
    assert result == "He..."

def test_no_truncation_needed_equal_length():
    s = "Hello"
    result = truncate(s, 5)
    assert result == "Hello"

def test_no_truncation_needed_shorter():
    s = "Hi"
    result = truncate(s, 5)
    assert result == "Hi"

def test_custom_suffix():
    s = "abcdef"
    result = truncate(s, 4, "..")
    assert result == "ab.."

def test_suffix_longer_than_max_len():
    s = "abcdef"
    # max_len < len(suffix) leads to negative slicing
    # Expected behavior: s[:negative] becomes empty string
    result = truncate(s, 2, "...")
    assert result == "..."

def test_empty_string():
    assert truncate("", 5) == ""

def test_zero_max_len():
    # max_len = 0, suffix length > 0 -> s[:0 - suffix_len] -> s[-3] -> empty slice -> suffix
    assert truncate("Hello", 0) == "..."

def test_negative_max_len():
    # Negative max_len should slice from the end before adding suffix
    # Example: max_len = -1, suffix = "...", slice index = -1 - 3 = -4
    result = truncate("Hello", -1)
    assert result == "o..."

def test_suffix_empty():
    s = "Hello"
    result = truncate(s, 3, "")
    assert result == "Hel"

def test_suffix_equal_to_max_len():
    s = "HelloWorld"
    result = truncate(s, 3, "...")
    # slice index = 3 - 3 = 0, so "" + "..."
    assert result == "..."
